actions.thought: In heads-up play, KQo is a premium hand. Being in position (Button/SB), I want to take the initiative and build the pot. Limping allows the Big Blind to realize equity for free with weak hands. A standard min-raise to 200 is effective here to extract value and define ranges while keeping the pot manageable relative to stack depth (100BB).

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong hand well above the average starting hand. The opponent has made a standard min-raise from the button. While I could 3-bet to take control of the pot, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity without bloating the pot out of position in the very first hand of the tournament. I need to call 100 more chips to see the flop.

Flash is first to act on a flop of 8-6-T after defending the Big Blind. Flash holds Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). On this coordinated board, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction and manage the pot size out of position.

The flop comes 8h 6s Td. This board texture connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's defending range, which includes many straight draws (e.g., 79) and pairs (Tx, 8x, 6x). Although I have a range advantage with overpairs, my specific hand (KQo) currently has only overcards and very weak backdoor straight potential. Betting here risks getting check-raised or called by better hands, forcing me to fold or play a bloated pot with marginal equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot size, and keeps my range protected. I can comfortably call a bet on many turns or value bet if I hit a King or Queen.

On the turn Queen of Clubs, I still have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw. The pre-flop aggressor checked back on the flop, which might indicate a lack of a strong hand or a desire to control the pot. By checking again, I am playing defensively out of position and allowing my opponent to lead if they have improved. I can still potentially win at showdown with Ace-high if the hand checks down, or I might hit my straight on the river.

I have improved to Top Pair with a King kicker on the Turn. After checking back the flop, my hand is under-represented, and the opponent has shown weakness by checking twice. There are straights possible (J9, 97) on this board, but I cannot play fearfully with such a strong holding in a heads-up pot. I need to bet to extract value from worse pairs (Tx, 8x, 6x) and charge draws. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot (250) balances getting value while keeping the price attractive for worse hands to call.

Flash has picked up an open-ended straight draw (needing a 7 or J) on the turn Queen. Pro's bet of 250 into a 400 pot after checking the flop suggests they may have a marginal hand or a Queen, but could also be semi-bluffing. Given the pot odds and the strong draw, calling is the correct mathematical play to see if the river completes the straight or if Ace-high remains competitive.

On the river 7c, the board is 8h-6s-Td-Qc-7c. My 9c in hand completes a straight (6-7-8-9-10). This is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. Pro checked the flop and bet the turn, suggesting they might have a Queen, a Ten, or a missed draw that just improved to two-pair or a set. While J-9 would make a higher straight, it is statistically less likely than the range of hands I can get value from, such as Qx, TT, 88, or two-pair. I will lead with a value bet of 600, which is two-thirds of the pot, to extract maximum value from these hands.

On the River, the board reads 8h 6s Td Qc 7c. The 7 is a significant scare card because it fills the gap between the 6, 8, and T, meaning any player holding a 9 now has a straight (6-7-8-9-T). As the Big Blind, my opponent's range is very wide and heavily saturated with 9x hands (J9, T9, 98, 97, 96, etc.), many of which would have check-called the Turn with a pair or draw. The opponent's line—checking and calling the Turn, then leading out (donk betting) 2/3 pot on this specific River card—strongly suggests that their draw has completed or they have improved to two pair or better. While I have Top Pair with a good kicker (KQ), I lose to all straights, sets, and two-pair combinations. I beat only a pure bluff or misplayed marginal hand. Without reads in Hand #1, and given that the runout heavily favors the Big Blind's defending range, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve chips.

In a heads-up situation, Ace-Jack offsuit (AhJd) is a premium starting hand. Being on the button (Small Blind), I want to play aggressively to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage throughout the hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to build value and narrow the range of the Big Blind.

I hold Qd 4c in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the Button. In heads-up play, Q4o is a weak hand with poor playability, especially out of position. While my raw equity against a wide opening range might be mathematically sufficient for the direct pot odds (33%), the realizeable equity is much lower due to being out of position with deep stacks (approx 95BB). The 3x sizing makes defending with marginal hands less attractive compared to a min-raise. Defending Q4o is likely -EV, so the prudent play is to fold.

I hold Ace-King offsuit (As Kh) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My strategy is to build the pot immediately and seize the initiative. Limping is an option to trap, but raising is generally superior to extract value and deny equity to random hands in the Big Blind. A standard raise size of 2.5x the Big Blind (250) is appropriate to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable relative to stack sizes.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Seven offsuit. The opponent raised to 250 from the Button/Small Blind. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, with the pot offering me 3.33-to-1 odds (150 to win a total pot of 500). While T7o is a marginal hand and I will be out of position post-flop, it has sufficient equity against a wide heads-up button opening range to justify a defense at these odds. I will call and see how the flop develops.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a relatively dry board of 8-3-7. In heads-up play, middle pair is a solid hand to continue with, but being out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. I want to see how the opponent reacts. If they bet, I have a clear call to see a turn card. If they check behind, it's a good result for my marginal holding.

The flop comes 8d-3c-7s, a low, somewhat connected board that interacts better with the Big Blind's defending range (which contains many 8x, 7x, and straight draws like 56 or 69) than my high-card heavy range. While Ace-King high has good showdown value, betting here often isolates me against hands that beat me (pairs) or strong draws, while folding out hands I already dominate (QJ, J9). Checking back allows me to realize equity for free, keeps the opponent's range wide, protects my checking range, and controls the pot size. I can comfortably call a turn bet or delay c-bet if checked to again.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a board that just saw an Ace turn. After checking the flop and seeing the opponent check behind, the Ace is a scare card that likely hits a significant portion of the opponent's pre-flop opening range. Since I am out of position, I will check again to see how the opponent acts. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheap river if they also check.

The turn brings the Ace of Clubs, giving me top pair with a top kicker. This is an excellent card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, though it also acts as a scare card. After checking back the flop to control the pot on a texture that favored the Big Blind, I have now realized my equity and improved significantly. I must bet to extract value from second/third pairs (8x, 7x) that might call thinking I am bluffing the Ace, and to charge club draws or straight draws. Checking back would grant free cards to draws and miss a street of value. A bet of 300 (60% pot) balances value extraction while keeping the opponent's bluff-catching range engaged.

The Ace on the turn is a classic scare card that the pre-flop aggressor will frequently bet regardless of their actual holding. Having checked the flop, my middle pair of Sevens remains a competitive hand in a heads-up pot. The opponent's bet of 300 into 500 gives me pot odds of 2.67-to-1, meaning I need roughly 27% equity to continue. Given that the opponent could be betting many semi-bluffs or air cards that picked up equity or are simply using the Ace to represent strength, I have enough value to call and see the river. Folding here would be too exploitable.

On the river, the board is 8d-3c-7s-Ac-4h. I hold a pair of Sevens, which is a medium-strength showdown hand. The turn Ace was a scare card that the opponent bet after we both checked the flop. By calling the turn, I've indicated I have a piece of the board. The 4h on the river is a blank. There is little value in betting here, as better hands (like an Ace or an Eight) will call, and worse hands will likely fold. Checking is the best option to either reach a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from a missed draw or a smaller pocket pair.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsKh) on a river board of 8d-3c-7s-Ac-4h. The river 4h completes the 5-6 straight draw, which is well within the Big Blind's defending range. While I have a strong hand, betting is thin because I block the weaker Aces (Ax) that I would want to call me, and second/third pairs (8x, 7x) will likely fold to a second barrel on this runout. Furthermore, the opponent demonstrated a river check-raise line in Hand #1, making a bet risky if they are trapping with a straight or two pair. Checking back realizes my showdown value securely, ensuring I win against missed draws and smaller pairs without opening myself up to a difficult decision or losing value to a check-raise.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Deuce offsuit (J2o). While I have position post-flop, J2o is a very weak hand that doesn't play well in most scenarios. Given the stack depths and the early stage of the tournament, there is no need to defend or attack with such a marginal holding. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold 9d 4h on the Button (SB), which is a statistically very weak hand (trash). In a standard heads-up structure, 94o is near the bottom of the range and is a standard fold pre-flop. While I should be opening wide on the button, this specific hand has very poor playability and equity. There is no reason to get involved in a pot out of position post-flop (if BB calls) or waste chips bluffing this early in the match with such low equity. Folding is the most EV+ decision.

In heads-up play, King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand from the button. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and apply pressure to the Big Blind, aiming to either win the blinds immediately or play a pot with a range advantage and position.

I hold AQs in the Big Blind, a premium hand in Heads-Up play. The opponent has raised to 300 (3bb). While a 3-bet for value is a viable option to build the pot immediately, calling is strategically superior here for several reasons: 1) We are deep-stacked (~97bb effective), where flatting preserves the pot-to-stack ratio and allows for more maneuverability post-flop. 2) Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, including many dominated hands (weak Ax, Qx, Kx) that might fold to a 3-bet. 3) Being Out of Position (OOP), keeping the pot smaller initially reduces variance and allows me to under-represent my hand strength, protecting my checking range on future streets. I will call the additional 200 to see a flop.

I hold AsQs on a board of 8h Jd 7s. I missed the flop, but I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. The board texture is somewhat coordinated and connects well with the opponent's button opening range (which contains many Jx, 9T, and pairs). Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) is not advisable as it folds out hands I dominate and builds a pot against a stronger range. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they continue. If they bet, I will evaluate based on the sizing; if they check, I get a free card to try and improve.

I have an overcard and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 for a straight). As the pre-flop aggressor, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain initiative, represent a strong hand like a Jack, and capitalize on my equity if called.

I hold AsQs on a Jd-8h-7s board. Facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600), I have two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. The pot odds are 3:1 (25% equity required), and against a wide button opening range, my hand retains sufficient equity to float. Folding is too tight given the strength of AQ high, and raising would overplay the hand by turning it into a bluff on a board that hits the opponent's range reasonably well. Calling allows me to realize equity if I hit an Ace or Queen on the turn or pick up a flush draw.

On the Turn, the Queen of Clubs gives me Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK). The board (8h Jd 7s Qc) is quite coordinated, with T9 completing a straight. Being Out of Position, leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as it allows the opponent to fold their bluffs and weak hands while continuing only with hands that beat me or have strong equity. By checking, I allow the opponent to continue their aggression (double barrel) with draws or worse value hands, or to bluff with air. I plan to check-call a reasonable bet to keep the pot manageable against potential straights or two-pair hands while extracting value from weaker Queens or Jacks.

The Queen on the turn is an excellent scare card that favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Pro has checked for a second time, which often indicates a marginal hand like a middle pair or a weak Jack that will struggle to call further aggression. I will bet 800 into the 1200 pot as a semi-bluff. This bet puts maximum pressure on Pro's range while I maintain significant equity with a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (any 9) and a King as an overcard. If called, I have outs to improve on the river; if Pro folds, I take down a substantial pot immediately.

On the Turn, the Queen of Clubs gives me Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK). However, the board (8h Jd 7s Qc) is wet, with T9 completing a straight and several two-pair combinations (QJ, Q8, 78) possible. The opponent is double-barreling with a substantial size (800 into 1200), which polarizes their range. Raising here would likely only get action from hands that beat me (straights, sets, two pair) while folding out their bluffs. Calling is the optimal play to keep their bluffing range wide, control the size of the pot, and evaluate the river. I am well ahead of semi-bluffs and weaker value hands like KQ or AJ.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsQs) on a board of 8h Jd 7s Qc 8c. The river pairs the 8, which introduces full house possibilities for sets, though it reduces the likelihood of the opponent holding an 8 (trips) due to card removal. The straight (T9) completed on the turn remains a threat. Given the opponent's aggression (triple barrel potential), leading out (donk betting) is suboptimal as it isolates me against their value range while folding out their bluffs. Checking is the standard play to induce bluffs from missed draws or allow the opponent to check back with worse value hands (like KQ or AJ). I intend to check and evaluate their sizing if they bet.

The river 8c pairs the board. I missed my straight draw and am left with King-high, which has almost no showdown value. However, Pro has check-called three streets, including a significant bet on the turn. This sequence suggests Pro has a made hand, likely a Jack or a Queen, and has decided to go into bluff-catching mode. While a large river bet might occasionally force a fold from a weak Jack, the risk of being called by a sticky opponent is too high given the current board texture and the pot size. I will check behind and concede the pot, preserving my stack for a more favorable situation.

I hold 92o, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Heads-Up No-Limit Hold'em due to its lack of connectivity and low card rank. Despite having the Button (position post-flop), the hand falls into the bottom percentiles of starting hands that should be folded even in a wide heads-up opening range. Attempting to play this hand often leads to negative expected value, especially if faced with a 3-bet or resistance post-flop. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Six of diamonds, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy is to raise to gain initiative and build the pot. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind and capitalize on my positional advantage.

I hold Qd Td in the Big Blind. My opponent raised to 300 (3BB). This is a strong suited one-gapper that plays very well post-flop in heads-up scenarios. I am getting 2:1 pot odds on a call (calling 200 to win a pot of 600). My hand has sufficient equity to defend against a standard button opening range. 3-betting is an option, but calling is standard to keep the pot manageable while playing out of position.

I hold Qd Td on a flop of 2c Ah 6h. I have missed the flop completely, holding only Queen-high with no immediate straight or flush draws. The Ace-high board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Being out of position, leading out (donk betting) is ill-advised as I have no equity to back it up. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold, but for now, I check to see what they do.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Sixes) on a board with a heart flush draw. My opponent checked, and I need to bet for value and protection. I want to extract chips from weaker Aces, flush draws, or middle pairs that might be sticky. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot represents roughly two-thirds of the pot, which is a standard sizing for this board texture.

I hold Queen-high (Qd Td) on a board of 2c Ah 6h. I have no pair and no meaningful draws. The Ace-high texture favors the pre-flop aggressor significantly. My opponent has fired a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 600 (66% pot). Being out of position with a very weak hand and facing a substantial bet size, I do not have the pot odds or equity to justify a call. Floating is too risky given the board texture. The correct play is to fold and preserve my chip stack.

In heads-up play, K7s is a strong enough hand to open from the Button (Small Blind). It has good equity, suited potential, and high card strength. Raising allows me to take the initiative and utilize position post-flop. A standard min-raise to 200 puts pressure on the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable if called.

I am holding pocket sixes in the Big Blind. The opponent opened for a standard raise to 2x the big blind (200). 66 is a strong hand in heads-up play. While calling is an option to set-mine, I will 3-bet to 600 to take control of the hand, capitalize on my hand strength, and potentially end the hand pre-flop. If called, I have a hand that often maintains equity or can flop a set.

I opened K7s from the Button, which is a standard play in heads-up. The opponent (Flash) has 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind. This is the first time Flash has 3-bet pre-flop; in previous hands (Hand #1 and #3), he played passively from the BB by calling. This shift to aggression suggests a stronger hand range. Although I have position and suited cards, K7s is easily dominated by better Kings or Aces in a 3-bet pot. The pot odds are roughly 33%, but against a value-heavy range implied by his previous passive play, my equity is likely lower, and reverse implied odds are high. Folding is the prudent decision here to preserve my stack lead.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten suited, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand. This is a standard opening size that applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable if my opponent decides to defend their big blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 3c facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button (Flash). While 32s has some playability due to flush and straight potential, it is the lowest suited connector and suffers from poor equity against a standard button opening range, especially when out of position. The pot odds are 2:1, requiring about 33% equity, which this hand barely meets against a wide range, but realizing that equity out of position is difficult. Given the larger 3x raise sizing compared to a min-raise, defending becomes less profitable. Folding prevents bleeding chips in a marginal spot.

I hold Ten-Five offsuit (T5o) on the Button/Small Blind. This is a statistically weak hand (bottom 20% range) with poor playability due to being unsuited and disconnected. My opponent, Flash, has demonstrated aggressive tendencies, including defending their Big Blind and 3-betting my opens (as seen in Hand #9). Given the deep stack depth (over 100 BBs) and the lack of necessity to steal with trash hands, the most positive EV play is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with 2c 6s, which is a very weak hand. Given that I am in the Small Blind/Button position and only need to call 50 more to see a flop, I could consider a limp or a raise, but against a solid opponent like 'Pro' who has shown aggression, playing such a weak hand out of position post-flop isn't ideal. However, folding for only 50 chips is usually too tight in heads-up play. But looking at the hand quality, 26o is near the bottom of the range. I will fold to keep my stack preserved for better spots.

I hold J8o on the Button (SB), which is in the top 55% of hands and standard to open in Heads-Up play. I have folded my last three button opportunities (Hands 5, 7, 11), establishing a tighter image which may discourage the opponent from 3-betting light, despite their aggression in Hand #9. Raising to 2bb allows me to take the initiative and utilize my position post-flop.

Pro has opened for a minimum raise of 2x (200 total) from the button. I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. The pot odds are 3-to-1, meaning I only need 25% equity to make a profitable call. Given that button opening ranges in heads-up play are typically very wide, a hand containing a King has sufficient equity to defend. I will call to see the flop rather than 3-betting, as K3o does not play particularly well as a bluff and is likely behind Pro's continuing range if I raise.

On this Queen-high board, I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. Pro has checked behind or I am first to act; since the prompt indicates the pot is 400 and I have no chips to call, it is my turn to act. Checking is the standard play here as my hand has some showdown value against complete air but isn't strong enough to bet for value. I will check and see how the turn develops.

I hold J8o on a Qc 9c 2h board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and one overcard to the middle pair. The board texture is wet with a flush draw present and connected high cards, which hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well (Qx, 9x, flush draws). Since I have position, checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit the straight on the turn without risking a check-raise. C-betting is an option, but given the opponent's capability to check-raise (demonstrated in Hand #6) and the fact that my hand has very little showdown value currently, pot control is the preferred line.

On the turn, a second deuce has appeared, pairing the board. I currently hold King-high with a backdoor club draw that has now become a direct nut flush draw if a club hits on the river. Pro checked back on the flop, which often indicates a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to build a large pot, or a weak hand giving up. My King-high still has some showdown value against his total bluffs, but checking is the most prudent move here. It allows me to see a free river card to potentially hit my King or a club, and keeps the pot small in case Pro has a marginal Queen or a Nine. If Pro bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the standard play.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 2d (9c Qc 2h 2d). The action has gone check-check on the flop, and the opponent has checked to me again. I still hold a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten). While a delayed continuation bet could take down the pot against Ace-high or small pairs, my hand has sufficient equity to warrant seeing a free river. Betting risks a check-raise which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my straight draw equity for free and potentially win at showdown or evaluate the river action if I miss.

The board has paired deuces on the turn and a brick five on the river. The action has gone check-check on the flop and check-check on the turn. This sequence suggests both players have hands with some showdown value or are giving up. My King-high has decent showdown value in this specific spot against a range that includes many missed straight draws and lower high cards. Betting is unlikely to fold out an Ace or a Nine, and I'm ahead of most hands that would fold. Checking allows me to get to a cheap showdown or potentially catch a bluff if Pro decides to bet his missed draws. Therefore, I will check.

On the River, the board is [9c Qc 2h 2d 5h]. I hold Jack-high (J8o). The action checked through on the Flop and Turn. Flash checks to me again on the River. My hand has very little showdown value, beating only missed straight/flush draws with lower cards (e.g., T7, 76, random clubs). 

Flash's line of checking three streets suggests a marginal hand with showdown value (like Ace-high, a small pocket pair, or a weak 9/2/5) that is happy to see a cheap showdown, or a complete air hand. If I bet, I am trying to fold out Ace-high or King-high. However, given my own passive line (checking back flop and turn), my range is capped and does not represent much strength. A sudden bet on the river might look like a bluff, and Flash might look me up with Ace-high or any pair. 

Since the pot is small (400 chips, 4 BBs), the reward for bluffing is low compared to the risk of being called by a hand that was planning to check-call. Checking back allows me to preserve my stack and potentially win against a worse bust, though I expect to lose to Ace-high often. It is the variance-minimizing and likely +EV play compared to bluffing into a 'showdown mode' range.

I have King-Jack offsuit on the button, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My strategy so far has involved consistent 3x opens when entering the pot from the button. Raising to 300 allows me to take control of the hand with a range advantage and position. Pro has folded several times to pre-flop raises, and KJo is well ahead of an average defending range.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit (Qs Ah), which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. While a 3-bet for value is a standard option here, calling is also very strong as it keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue with dominated hands like Ax and Qx, and avoids bloating the pot out of position while 100BB deep. In Hand #6, I successfully played AsQs by calling a 300 raise, so I will take a similar low-variance line here to realize my equity post-flop.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit on a Th-3d-8d flop. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser, Flash. I have two overcards and reasonable showdown value but no made hand or draw. The standard play in this spot is to check to the aggressor to see if they continue with a continuation bet. Leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary and allows the opponent to play perfectly. Checking gives me the option to call a reasonable bet or see a free turn if they check back.

I have King-high with overcards and a gutshot straight draw on a Ten-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will continue with a standard continuation bet of half the pot. This exerts pressure on the opponent's weaker holdings and allows me to maintain control of the hand. My Jd also acts as a useful blocker for some diamond flush draws.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, giving me two overcards to the Ten-high board (Th-3d-8d). In a heads-up scenario, AQ high often holds significant showdown value against a wide Button opening range that includes many weaker hands and draws. The opponent has made a standard continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 600 (half-pot), offering me 3:1 odds. To break even on a call, I need approximately 25% equity. Against a range consisting of pairs, flush draws, straight draws, and air, my hand has sufficient equity to continue. Folding the top of my unclamping range here would be exploitable, and raising isolates me against a stronger range. Calling allows me to realize my equity and evaluate the turn.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Ten of Clubs (Th 3d 8d Tc), making it less likely the opponent holds a Ten due to card removal. I hold Ace-Queen high, which still has reasonable showdown value against a wide Button opening range and missed flush draws. Leading out (donk betting) here serves little purpose as it folds out the bluffs I beat and isolates me against better hands. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor who has position. If the opponent checks back, I see a free river and realize my equity. If they bet, I can re-evaluate based on sizing, but for now, checking is the correct low-variance play.

The turn card pairs the top card of the flop, which is generally a good card for the pre-flop aggressor. However, I currently only have King-high. While I have some showdown value and outs to improve (Kings and Jacks), checking back here allows me to control the pot size and see a free river card. Pro's history suggests they can be quite tight, but they also called a significant turn bet in Hand #6. By checking, I also protect my checking range, making it harder for Pro to put me on a specific hand. If the river is a blank, I might still win at showdown or can decide whether to bluff based on the final board state.

On the river, the board is [Th 3d 8d Tc Kd]. I hold [Qs Ah], giving me a pair of Tens with Ace-King-Queen kickers (effectively Ace-high showdown value). The King on the river is an overcard that connects with many hands in the opponent's range (Kx) and completes a potential flush if they hold two diamonds. Since the opponent checked back the turn, their range likely consists of marginal made hands (8x, 3x, pocket pairs), draws, or Kx. I lose to any pair and beat only missed straight draws or lower Ace-highs. Leading out would only get called by better hands and fold out worse ones. Checking is the best option to potentially see a cheap showdown or evaluate if the opponent decides to bluff.

I have improved to top pair (Kings) on the river, complementing the Tens on the board for a strong two-pair hand. Pro has checked on both the turn and the river, which suggests a capped range or a hand with modest showdown value, such as an Eight or a small pocket pair. While a flush is possible given the three diamonds, Pro's passive line makes it less likely they are holding a monster. I will bet 600 (half the pot) to extract value from their middle-pair holdings or missed draws that might attempt a hero call.

On the river, the board is [Th 3d 8d Tc Kd]. The King of Diamonds completes the flush draw that was present on the flop, which is a significant portion of the opponent's range given the 'bet flop, check turn' line. The opponent bets 600 into 1200 (half pot). My hand is Ace-high (effectively playing the board's pair of Tens with my Ace-King-Queen kickers). I lose to any pocket pair, any 3, 8, T, or K, and any flush. I have completely missed this flop with 6-high and no draws. The board (Tc-Kc-Jd) is extremely coordinated and favors the pre-flop raiser's range of broadway cards. I will check and intend to fold to any bet as I have zero equity.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsKh) on a very coordinated and wet flop (Tc Kc Jd). I have 6-high with no draws on a very coordinated board (Tc-Kc-Jd-3d). Pro checked back the flop, which might indicate a weak hand, but my hand has zero showdown value. I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AsKh) on a very coordinated board (Tc Kc Jd 3d) that connects well with the Big Blind's defending range (including straights, two pairs, and sets). The board runout is Ten-King-Jack-Three-Six. I am on the button with 6c3s, which is a very weak hand. The pot is small (150 total) and I am facing a 50-chip call from the small blind. I hold Pocket Tens (Tc Th), which is a premium pair in heads-up play. My goal is to extract value and build a pot. I am in the Big Blind holding Ten of Spades and Deuce of Clubs (T2o). Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. I am on the button with T6o in a heads-up situation. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard opening size that allows me to win the blinds immediately or play the hand with positional advantage on later streets if called.

In a heads-up match, holding A2o in the Big Blind facing a 3bb open (raise to 300) is a standard defense. I am getting 2:1 pot odds, requiring roughly 33% equity to call profitably. Against a typical wide button opening range, A2o has significantly more equity than this (around 45-55%). While I will be out of position post-flop, the hand is too strong to fold. On a wet flop of Kc Jc 9s, I hold the nut flush draw with Ac 2h. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Flash), which contains many broadway combinations (KQ, KJ, KT, QJ, etc.). Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here. Checking is the standard line to allow the opponent to c-bet, which Flash has done frequently in previous hands. If Flash bets, I have a strong enough hand to continue via call or check-raise. If Flash checks back, I realize equity for free.

I am the dealer (Flash) and I opened for 300 pre-flop with T6o, and Pro called. The flop is Kc Jc 9s, giving me a gutshot straight draw (any Queen makes a straight). The board is quite coordinated and likely hits my opponent's calling range from the Big Blind. However, since the opponent checked to me, I will place a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is a standard play here to represent a strong hand (like a King or a Jack) and to potentially take the pot down now, while maintaining equity with my straight draw.

I hold the Ac 2h on a Kc Jc 9s board, giving me the nut flush draw and an overcard. My opponent, Flash, has c-bet 300 (half-pot) into a pot of 600. Raising is an option but risks bloating the pot out of position against a range that connects very well with this board (sets, two pairs, straights). Calling is the standard, lower-variance play to realize my equity.

I hold the Nut Flush (Ac) on a paired board (Kc Jc 9s 9c). While the paired board introduces Full House risks (which beat my flush), my hand is extremely strong and at the top of my range. Being out of position, leading (donk betting) often folds out the opponent's bluffs and weaker made hands (like one-pair hands that are now scared). Checking is the standard optimal play here to allow the aggressive opponent (Flash) to fire a second barrel with worse flushes, trips, straights, or bluffs. If Flash bets, I can decide whether to call or check-raise for value. If Flash checks back, I can value bet the river.

The turn 9c pairs the board and introduces a third club. I have the Tc, which gives me both a gutshot straight draw (any Queen) and a flush draw. Pro has checked for the second time. Checking back allows me to take a free card to realize my significant equity without risking a check-raise that would force me to fold my draws. If I hit the river, I can then value bet or bluff depending on the card and Pro's action.

I hold the Nut Flush with Ac on a paired board (Kc Jc 9s 9c 2d). To extract value from these hands, I must lead out rather than check. A half-pot bet (600) is small enough to induce a call from a King or a smaller flush but large enough to get meaningful value.

On the river, the board is Kc-Jc-9s-9c-2d. I hold 10c-6h, giving me Ten-high with the pair of Nines on the board. My opponent (Pro) has led out for 600 into an 1800 pot. I hold Ts6c on the Button (SB). This is a weak, unsuited hand (offsuit ten-six) with poor playability and below-average equity against a random hand. I am holding 23o, which is statistically the weakest hand in poker. While I am on the button in a heads-up scenario, this hand has very little playability post-flop and poor showdown value. I am in the Small Blind with T7o, a marginal hand. The opponent (Flash) has a high defense frequency in the Big Blind, calling 4 out of my 6 previous raises and 3-betting once. I am in the Small Blind with 6-4 offsuit. While this is a heads-up game where ranges are wider, 6-4 offsuit is quite weak. I am holding Ace-King suited (AsKs), a premium starting hand, on the Button (Small Blind). My stack is 109BB deep. I have hit middle pair (nines) on a board of 2h-Td-9d. This is a decent piece of the board, but the texture is quite connected with many straight and flush draws available. I hold AsKs (Ace-King suited) on the Button and raised pre-flop. The flop comes 2h-Td-9d. This texture hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well (pairs, straight draws, flush draws). I have middle pair (nines) on a somewhat coordinated board. After checking the flop and seeing Pro check back, it's possible Pro has a marginal hand or a draw. I hold AsKs on the Turn with the board reading 2h-Td-9d-6s. After checking back the flop, the pot remains small (500). The opponent has checked to me again. I have two pair, Nines and Sixes, with a Ten kicker. The river card (6d) completed a potential diamond flush and paired the board. I am on the button with Q9o, a strong hand for heads-up play. Pro has shown a pattern of folding to pre-flop raises from the button (Hands 2, 10). Raising to 300 is a standard play here to put pressure on the big blind and seize the initiative.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jd Js), which is a premium starting hand in Heads-Up play. I am in position on the Button. I am in the Big Blind and the flop is 9h-2d-5s. I hold Ace-high with a backdoor heart flush draw. This is a dry board where the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) will often continuation bet. I hold an overpair (JJ) on a dry, disconnected flop of 9h-2d-5s. I can comfortably call a turn bet or bet for value on later streets if checked to.

On a board of 9h-2d-5s-2c, my Ace-high (Ah6h) retains significant showdown value, especially after Pro checked back the flop. The turn 2c is a neutral card that doesn't change much. I checked back the flop with an overpair (JJ) to disguise my hand strength and pot control on a dry board. The turn is a 2c, pairing the board but changing little. Pro checked back the flop, which typically caps their range and suggests they don't have a strong 9 or an overpair. The river is the 4d, making the final board 9h-2d-5s-2c-4d. My Ace-high (Ah6h) has some showdown value here, especially considering Pro checked back the flop. I hold an overpair (JJ) on a final board of 9h-2d-5s-2c-4d. The river is a blank that doesn't complete any obvious draws. My opponent, Flash, has checked to me on the river after calling a turn bet. Pro checked back the flop, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a plan to delay a continuation bet. I am on the button with 8h 6c. In heads-up play, 86o is a decent hand to open for a raise to maintain pressure and exploit positional advantage. I hold AcTc (Ace-Ten suited) on the Button/Small Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. I am in the Big Blind with 2d 6s, which is an extremely weak hand. Pro has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Although I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, 26 offsuit is almost never profitable to play out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 8h. The opponent (Flash) has limped from the Small Blind. K8s is a respectable hand in heads-up play, having good high-card value and flush potential. I am in the Big Blind holding Kh8h. The pot was limped pre-flop. The flop is Ts7h3c, giving me King-high and a backdoor flush draw. I have flopped top pair with a Ten. While my kicker is weak (5), top pair in a heads-up, limped pot is very likely the best hand. My opponent checked, so I will bet half the pot to extract value from weaker hands like a 7, a 3, or straight draws, while also protecting my hand against overcards.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh8h on a Ts7h3c board. The pot was limped pre-flop. My opponent bet 100 (half pot) after I checked. I am in the Big Blind with Kh8h. The board is Ts 7h 3c Qc. On the flop, I check-called a bet of 100. I hold King-high (Kh8h) on a board of Ts 7h 3c Qc 6s. The pot is small (400) and was limped pre-flop. My opponent bet the flop but checked back the turn. I have top pair (Tens) with a weak kicker (5) on a board of Ts-7h-3c-Qc-6s. The pot was limped pre-flop. I bet the flop and was called, then checked the turn after the Queen appeared. On a board of [5s, 5c, Tc], I hold [8c, Jc] giving me a Jack-high flush draw. If they bet, I have sufficient equity to continue (check-call or potentially check-raise depending on sizing). Leading out (donk betting) is unnecessary and denies the opponent the chance to bluff or c-bet with a weaker hand. Therefore, I will check.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the flop of 5s-5c-Tc is relatively favorable for my range. I hold a Jack-high flush draw (Jc8c) on a paired board of 5s5cTc. The opponent has bet 300 into a pot of 600. This gives me pot odds of 3:1 (calling 300 to win 900), requiring roughly 25% equity to call. A raise (check-raise) is an option to leverage fold equity, but on a paired board, I risk getting committed against a full house or having to fold to a 3-bet, denying my equity. Calling keeps the opponent's bluffing range in the hand and is the lower-variance line to realize my strong equity. I will call to see the turn.

On the turn, the board is 5s 5c Tc 2h. The 2h is a blank that does not change the texture significantly. I still hold a Jack-high flush draw (Jc8c). The turn 2h is a complete brick and does not change the board texture. Pro checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. These hands are now in a very difficult spot. I'll bet 600, half the pot, to represent the Ace and force a fold from those better but non-Ace hands.

I have a pair of Nines on a board of 6-6-9-T-7. While I had top pair on the flop, the Ten on the turn is an overcard, and any Eight now completes a straight. I have Ace-Seven suited on the button. This is a very strong hand in heads-up play, especially against an opponent with a relatively short stack of 2,350. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and apply pressure.

I have Ace-Seven suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Pro has raised to 2,350, leaving only 100 chips behind, effectively committing their entire stack.
